svg.text: A formally valid argument reaches a false conclusion because one premise is false even though the structure is strong.
Start with the concrete case: A formally valid argument reaches a false conclusion because one premise is false even though the structure is strong.
Name the hidden structure: Grid separating structural validity from premise truth.
Watch for the trap: Assuming logical validity means the conclusion is true.
Transfer the habit immediately: Classify three arguments by validity and premise truth separately.

Unlike broad intro courses, this lesson turns validity vs truth into a practical operating skill through a real scenario, a visual model, a retrieval gate, and a transfer task built around argument mapping, evidence discipline, and fair-minded judgment.
Assuming logical validity means the conclusion is true.

Classify three arguments by validity and premise truth separately.
The goal is not to memorize the label, but to practice a repeatable move in real judgment. Ask the learner to produce a concrete artifact, not just an opinion.
